1.synthesis: Ezekiel is told to shave his head and beard with a sword as a razor, weigh the hair, and divide it: a third burned in the city, a third struck with the sword around it, a third scattered to the wind — with a few hairs bound in his garment, some of which are then burned (vv.1–4). The integrative thread is the enacted threefold destruction of Jerusalem with a preserved-yet-threatened remnant: the shaved and divided hair dramatizes the fates awaiting the city’s population — plague, sword, and dispersion — while the few hairs kept and yet partly burned signal that even the remnant is not exempt from further refining judgment, the sign-act making the coming catastrophe vivid and certain.
1.historical_context: Shaving the head and beard (v.1) was itself a sign of mourning and humiliation. The threefold division of the hair — burned, struck, scattered (v.2) — dramatizes the threefold fate of Jerusalem’s inhabitants in the siege: famine/plague, sword, and exile (Ezekiel 5:12). The few hairs bound in the garment, some still burned (vv.3–4), depict a remnant that yet faces further judgment.
1.literary_structure: This sign-act (vv.1–4) completes the dramatized-judgment series, the shaved and divided hair vividly portraying the coming destruction. The preserved-yet-burned remnant (vv.3–4) introduces the remnant theme, and the interpretation follows in Ezekiel 5:5.
1.theological_application: God’s judgment is thorough and discriminating, portrayed in the divided hair (v.2); sin’s consequences are real and severe (Galatians 6:7). Yet the preserved remnant (v.3) shows mercy at work even in judgment, though it too must be refined. The believer is sobered that God deals seriously with sin and warned not to presume that belonging to a remnant exempts from his refining work (1 Peter 4:17), while comforted that God preserves a people through judgment. God’s judgment is thorough and discriminating, portrayed in the divided hair (v.2); sin’s consequences are real and severe (Galatians 6:7). Yet the preserved remnant (v.3) shows mercy at work even in judgment, though it too must be refined. The believer is sobered that God deals seriously with sin and warned not to presume that belonging to a remnant exempts from his refining work (1 Peter 4:17), while comforted that God preserves a people through judgment.
1.christology: The thorough judgment dramatized in the razor sign (v.2) is the judgment Christ bore for his people, that the refining fire might fall on him rather than them (Isaiah 53:5). The preserved remnant (v.3) points to the “remnant chosen by grace” (Romans 11:5) gathered and kept in Christ, who refines his people like gold (Malachi 3:3) and loses none the Father gives him (John 6:39).

5.synthesis: God interprets the sign: “This is Jerusalem. I have set her in the center of the nations,” yet she rebelled more wickedly than the nations around her (vv.5–6). Therefore God himself is against her: parents and children will eat one another, a third will die of plague, a third by sword, a third scattered (vv.8–12), “and they shall know that I am the LORD” (v.13). The integrative thread is the heightened judgment on privileged Jerusalem for her greater rebellion, aimed at the knowledge of God: set at the center of the nations as a witness, Jerusalem sinned worse than the pagans, so her judgment is correspondingly severe — and its ultimate purpose, repeated throughout Ezekiel, is that all “shall know that I am the LORD.”
5.historical_context: Jerusalem “set in the center of the nations” (v.5) was meant to be a witness to the peoples, yet rebelled more than they (v.6), incurring greater guilt. The threefold judgment (v.12) matches the divided hair. The cannibalism (v.10) is the covenant-curse extremity (Deuteronomy 28:53). The refrain “they shall know that I am the LORD” (v.13) — occurring scores of times in Ezekiel — names the purpose of God’s acts.
5.literary_structure: This section (vv.5–17) interprets the razor sign, identifying Jerusalem (v.5) and detailing her judgment for greater-than-pagan rebellion. The recognition formula “they shall know that I am the LORD” (v.13) is the theological refrain of the whole book, here grounding judgment in God’s self-revelation.
5.theological_application: Greater privilege brings greater accountability: Jerusalem, set as a witness among the nations, was judged more severely for sinning more than they (vv.6–8; Luke 12:48; Amos 3:2). The ultimate purpose of God’s judgments is that people “shall know that I am the LORD” (v.13) — his self-revelation and glory. The believer is warned that spiritual privilege heightens responsibility, and reminded that even God’s judgments serve the great end of making him known (Ezekiel 6:7). Greater privilege brings greater accountability: Jerusalem, set as a witness among the nations, was judged more severely for sinning more than they (vv.6–8; Luke 12:48; Amos 3:2). The ultimate purpose of God’s judgments is that people “shall know that I am the LORD” (v.13) — his self-revelation and glory. The believer is warned that spiritual privilege heightens responsibility, and reminded that even God’s judgments serve the great end of making him known.
5.christology: Jerusalem set at the center to witness yet failing (v.5) points to the One who is the true light to the nations (Isaiah 49:6; John 8:12), succeeding where the city failed. The judgment for greater privilege spurned (v.6) is the warning Christ presses on those who reject greater light (Matthew 11:23–24). And the purpose “they shall know that I am the LORD” (v.13) is fulfilled in Christ, in whose face the knowledge of the glory of God shines (2 Corinthians 4:6).
